The modern job market is undergoing a profound transformation, with Artificial Intelligence (AI) emerging as a pivotal force in recruitment and talent acquisition. Understanding this shift is paramount for job seekers aiming to secure roles, especially within the nuanced Australian community services sector. name: ATS Resume Formatting Checklist.docx
snippet:
ATS Resume Formatting Checklist
File Format & Structure:
[ ] Save as .docx or PDF (text-based): 
Avoid image-based PDFs.
If possible, use .docx.
[ ] Simple, Single-Column Layout: 
Avoid tables, text boxes, and multiple columns.
[ ] Standard Section Headings: 
Use clear headings like "Summary," "Work Experience," "Education," and "Skills."
Avoid creative or unusual headings.
[ ] Consistent Date Format: 
Use MM/YYYY or Month YYYY.

# Resume Writing Best Practices Guide

## Introduction

This guide covers key principles for writing an effective resume. Following these best practices helps create a clear, professional document that appeals to both human recruiters and Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS), increasing the chances of getting an interview.

## Formatting Best Practices

Good formatting makes your resume easy to read and scan.

* **Readability:** Use a clean, professional font (e.g., Calibri, Arial, Garamond) in a readable size (10-12 points). Ensure sufficient white space and clear section headings.
* **Consistency:** Keep formatting uniform throughout – use the same font style, size hierarchy for headings/body text, and style for bullet points.
* **Length:** Aim for one page if you have less than 10 years of experience. Two pages is acceptable for more experienced professionals, but keep it concise and relevant.
* **ATS-Friendliness:** Avoid complex formatting like tables, columns, images, or headers/footers, as ATS might struggle to parse them[cite: 160]. Use standard section headings (e.g., "Work Experience," "Education," "Skills"). Save as a .docx or PDF (ensure it's text-selectable).

I. The 2025 Australian Job Market: Navigating a Landscape of Growth and Transformation
The Australian job market in 2025 presents a complex but ultimately optimistic picture for discerning job seekers. It is characterized by overall resilience, specific sectoral growth, and an increasing interplay between technological advancement and evolving workforce needs. Understanding these dynamics is the first step towards a successful job search.
A. Economic Outlook and Labour Market Dynamics for 2025
The broader economic environment indicates a labour market that, while experiencing some shifts, remains fundamentally sound. Projections for 2025 suggest an unemployment rate stabilizing around 4.1% to 4.25%. Data from the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) for April 2025 showed the trend unemployment rate at 4.1%. Similarly, Advance Global Australians reported the national unemployment rate at 4.1% in March 2025, a modest rise from the previous year. KPMG's analysis indicated that after restrictive conditions peaked in early 2023 and eased towards neutral levels by mid-2024, the labour market returned marginally to the constrained zone in the December quarter of 2024, with the KPMG Labour Market Pressure Index climbing to 0.3 percentage points. Author: AI Architect
1. Introduction & Problem Statement
The modern job application process, particularly within specialized fields like the Australian Community Services sector, is a significant source of stress and inefficiency for job seekers. Applicants are expected to create highly tailored resumes, compelling cover letters, and detailed Key Selection Criteria (KSC) responses for each role. This process is time-consuming, requires expert-level writing skills, and is often complicated by the need to pass automated Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS).
Early-career professionals in this sector are passionate about their work but are often not expert writers or ATS specialists. They spend countless hours manually tailoring documents, leading to burnout and a lower quantity and quality of applications.
The Personal AI Resume Agent is designed to solve this problem. It will act as an expert co-pilot for the job seeker, automating the most tedious parts of the application process. By combining the user's professional history with a deep, sector-specific knowledge base, the agent will generate high-quality, ATS-compliant application documents in minutes, not hours.

snippet: # Prompt \& Context Engineering for Resume Agents: Best Practices and Solution-Design Template The most reliable way to turn a large-language-model (LLM) into a high-performing assistant is to master two complementary skills: **prompt engineering** (the art of giving precise instructions) and **context engineering** (the science of supplying every additional piece of information the model needs at exactly the right moment)[^1][^2][^3]. When both are applied systematically, small tweaks in wording, memory layout, retrieval and tool orchestration routinely deliver 10-30% accuracy gains and dramatic reductions in hallucination rates across hiring workloads[^4][^5]. Below you will find: - a research-driven synthesis of current best practice; - four visual guides that map prompt patterns, context-engineering tactics, retrieval pipelines and agent architecture; - a reusable **Agentic Solution-Design Requirements Template** that teams can fill in to give any AI system enough detail to build an end-to-end resume agent. ## 1 Why Resume Agents Fail Without Rigorous Context Most publicly available resume bots rely on a single prompt plus the raw PDF a job-seeker uploads. # Resume Best Practices & Style Guide (Australian Context)

This guide outlines the principles for crafting an effective, ATS-friendly, and impactful master resume specifically for the Australian job market, with a focus on Community Services and Social Work.

---

## 1. Overall Philosophy
* **Targeted & Relevant:** Customise for each role. Every point should demonstrate your fit.
* **Achievement-Oriented:** Focus on quantifiable results and impact, not just responsibilities.
* **Clear & Concise:** Easy to read and digest. Avoid jargon where plain language suffices.
* **Professional & Ethical:** Reflects the high ethical standards of the community services sector.
* **ATS Friendly:** Optimise for Applicant Tracking Systems.

---

## 2. Australian Context Specifics

* **Personal Information:**
    * **DO NOT INCLUDE:** Photos, date of birth, age, marital status, religion, nationality, or detailed home address (city/state/postcode or city/country is sufficient). These are generally discouraged to prevent unconscious bias.
    * **INCLUDE:** Full Name, Phone Number, Professional Email Address, LinkedIn Profile URL.
* **Resume Length:**
    * **Graduates/Entry-Level (0-2 years):** Max 1 page.
    * **Mid-Career (3-10 years):** 1-2 pages.

# **Leveraging Prompt Engineering and AI Tools for Effective Resume Customization**

## **Introduction**

The contemporary job market presents a significant challenge for applicants: tailoring resumes effectively for each specific job application. This process is time-consuming yet crucial for navigating Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) and capturing the attention of human recruiters. The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) and associated Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools offers a potential solution, promising to streamline and enhance resume customization. However, harnessing these tools effectively requires an understanding of prompt engineering – the practice of crafting inputs that guide AI towards desired outputs. This report explores the best practices for prompt engineering in the context of resume tailoring, examines how AI tools and prompt templates can assist job seekers, and addresses the inherent challenges and limitations of this approach. The goal is to provide job seekers with a comprehensive understanding of how to leverage AI responsibly and effectively to create compelling, targeted resumes that increase their chances of securing interviews.

name: keyword_optimisation_strategies.md.txt
snippet:
Resume Keywords and Optimization Strategies
Introduction: What are Resume Keywords?
Resume keywords are specific words and phrases that Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) and recruiters use to identify and filter candidates. These keywords are derived from job descriptions and reflect the essential skills, experience, qualifications, and attributes sought by employers.
Keywords can appear in various sections of a resume, with ATS often prioritizing:
	•	Company Values: Terms reflecting the company's mission and culture, such as "responsibility" and "sustainability."
	•	Job Titles: Specific job titles are crucial for matching candidates to roles. If your previous titles were uncommon, consider adjusting them to align with the target job description.
	•	Soft Skills: Personal attributes like "teamwork," "creativity," and "leadership qualities."
	•	Hard Skills: Practical and technical abilities, such as specific software proficiency (e.g., "Adobe Creative Suite," "Microsoft Excel," "MySQL") or use of specific equipment (e.g., "micrometer").
	•	Qualifications: Degrees, licenses, and industry-standard certifications.
	•	Software: Specific software programs relevant to the role. Ensure you use the exact and proper name of the software.
	•	Industry-Specific Skills or Language: Common terminology and jargon within a particular industry.

name: quantify accomplishments.md.txt
snippet:
Use Numbers and Statistics:
Instead of saying "assisted clients," state "provided direct support to 50 clients per month" or "increased client access to resources by 15%". 

Focus on Outcomes:
Instead of saying "developed a program," state "developed and implemented a program that resulted in a 20% reduction in recidivism rates". 

Highlight Specific Achievements:
Instead of saying "managed a team," state "managed a team of 10, increasing productivity by 20% within six months". 

Quantify Your Impact on Clients:
Mention the number of clients helped, the types of services provided, and the positive outcomes achieved. 

Showcase Your Skills:
Quantify your skills by mentioning the number of cases you handled, the complexity of the cases, and the types of interventions you used. 

Tailor Your Resume:
Customize your resume to match the specific requirements and keywords of each job description. 

Use Action Verbs:
Start your accomplishment statements with strong action verbs that demonstrate your accomplishments, such as "implemented," "developed," "managed," "increased," or "improved". 

Be Specific and Measurable:
Provide concrete examples of your achievements and the impact you made. 

Showcase your specialization areas:
Many social workers specialize in certain areas, such as child welfare, mental health, or substance abuse. Make sure to prominently feature your specialization(s) on your resume, as it shows your expertise and passion.

snippet:
Software Requirements Specification (SRS)
Personal AI Resume Agent v1.0
1. Introduction
1.1 Purpose
This document provides a complete and detailed specification of the requirements for the Personal AI Resume Agent v1.0. It is intended to serve as the primary source of truth for developers, outlining the system's functionalities, constraints, and expected behavior. The agent is designed as a set of executable Python scripts, optimized for use within the Google AI Studio environment.
1.2 Scope
The scope of this project encompasses the design and implementation of two core Python scripts and their associated knowledge artifacts:
populate_profile.py: A one-time setup script to build a user's professional profile from their existing documents.
run_agent.py: The main script that takes a job URL as input and generates a full set of tailored application documents (resume, cover letter, KSC responses) and an ATS analysis.
Knowledge Base: A set of structured text and JSON files that provide sector-specific expertise to the agent.
Database: A local, file-based SQLite database to store the user's persistent professional profile.
Out of Scope:
A continuously running web server (e.g., FastAPI, Flask).
A graphical user interface (HTML, CSS, JS).

Your resume is a key tool in helping to get you in the running for a role. When youﬁrst apply for a job, you’ve just got words on paper to impress with—so it’s crucialto use those words wisely. Amanda Buxton, Director at recruitment ﬁrm BuxtonPratt Consulting, shares her tips on how to get the tone and language of yourresume just right.
Use active voice
Your resume should grab your reader’s attention as quickly as possible, and usingactive voice instead of passive voice will engage your reader faster.
“Passive sentences tend to be wordier and more diﬃcult to read, and are not asimpactful as active ones,” Buxton says. For example: “A promotion to team leaderwas awarded to me after only six months of service,” is not as direct as the activeform, “I was promoted to team leader after only six months.”
Scan your resume for passive sentences and replace them with active sentences tocreate more impact, Buxton says.
 Beware buzzwords
Popular, overused words such as ‘energetic’, ‘driven’, ‘motivated’, ‘enthusiastic’,‘hardworking’, ‘team player’, ‘loyal’, ‘client-focussed’ or ‘achievement-oriented’should be treated with caution.
“If you do use these words you should make sure you can back them up with solidexamples,” Buxton says.

Leveraging Prompt Engineering and AI Tools for Effective Resume Customization
Introduction
The contemporary job market presents a significant challenge for applicants: tailoring resumes effectively for each specific job application. This process is time-consuming yet crucial for navigating Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) and capturing the attention of human recruiters. The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) and associated Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools offers a potential solution, promising to streamline and enhance resume customization. However, harnessing these tools effectively requires an understanding of prompt engineering – the practice of crafting inputs that guide AI towards desired outputs. This report explores the best practices for prompt engineering in the context of resume tailoring, examines how AI tools and prompt templates can assist job seekers, and addresses the inherent challenges and limitations of this approach. The goal is to provide job seekers with a comprehensive understanding of how to leverage AI responsibly and effectively to create compelling, targeted resumes that increase their chances of securing interviews.
Understanding Prompt Engineering for Large Language Models (LLMs)
Prompt engineering is the art and science of designing and optimizing the inputs (prompts) given to AI models, particularly LLMs, to elicit specific, desired responses. It involves carefully crafting instructions, providing context, and sometimes offering examples to

One simple trick to make your resume stand out
When you sit down to update your CV, do you start by jotting down a few points about your duties and responsibilities
in your current role – points that could easily have come from your job description? While this may seem like a logical
place to start, it’s rarely an approach that creates a winning CV.
On your CV – and in any discussion with a recruiter or interview with a hiring manager for that matter – it is your
achievements and results that really count. In other words, we don’t want to read what you think about yourself; we
want proof that you did your previous jobs well.
After all, anyone can say they are innovative, but not everyone can say they designed and delivered a new online sales
booking system that increased sales by 15 per cent in the ﬁrst six months!
Use action verbs

What to consider when writing a prompt
When writing an AI prompt, there are four key areas to focus on: Persona, task, context, and format.
The persona refers to the information you provide about yourself when writing an AI prompt. You could
write a simple prompt like “Write an email to [contact] welcoming them to the company,” but you can
add more context with a persona in order to get a better response. Instead, try “I am an HR manager.
Write an email to [contact] welcoming them to the company. Invite them to schedule a meeting with
me on [date].”
The task refers to the actual task you’re telling AI to perform. You should be specific about your task
instructions to get the best results. However, it’s also important to be clear and concise when
assigning AI tasks. For example, your task could be “Write an email to [contact] to schedule a meeting
on [day].” If you want to include anything specific in your email, you can add context once

Tailor your resume.

name: cover-letter-sentence-starters.docx
snippet:
Cover Letter Sentence Starters

Because knowing what to say in a cover letter can be difficult, we’ve listed a number of “sentence starters” to aid you in matching your unique background, skill set and qualifications to the needs of the position and employer. Use these sentence starters as examples when drafting the three parts of your cover letter: the introduction, the body and the closing paragraph.

INTRODUCTION
It is with great interest that I am applying for the position of______.
(Name of contact) provided me with your name and suggested I contact you in reference to the _____   position.
As a recent graduate of SUNY-ESF with a degree in _______ and an avid (reader, writer, sports enthusiast, etc.), I believe I am a strong applicant for the position of ________.
As a (research, congressional, etc.) intern with (name of organization) I gained valuable experience in _______ that makes me an ideal candidate for the position of _______.
I am eager to apply for the position of ________ because I have the _______ that it takes to be successful in the position.
Thank you for speaking with me on (date) and discussing the ______ position. I am very interested in ________.
Your organization impresses me because of (the performance of your product, the integrity of your support staff, its customer satisfaction, etc.).

# Resume Writing Best Practices Guide

## Introduction

This guide covers key principles for writing an effective resume. Following these best practices helps create a clear, professional document that appeals to both human recruiters and Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS), increasing the chances of getting an interview.

## Formatting Best Practices

Good formatting makes your resume easy to read and scan.

* **Readability:** Use a clean, professional font (e.g., Calibri, Arial, Garamond) in a readable size (10-12 points). Ensure sufficient white space and clear section headings.
* **Consistency:** Keep formatting uniform throughout – use the same font style, size hierarchy for headings/body text, and style for bullet points.
* **Length:** Aim for one page if you have less than 10 years of experience. Two pages is acceptable for more experienced professionals, but keep it concise and relevant.
* **ATS-Friendliness:** Avoid complex formatting like tables, columns, images, or headers/footers, as ATS might struggle to parse them[cite: 160]. Use standard section headings (e.g., "Work Experience," "Education," "Skills"). Save as a .docx or PDF (ensure it's text-selectable).

# **Leveraging Prompt Engineering and AI Tools for Effective Resume Customization**

## **Introduction**

The contemporary job market presents a significant challenge for applicants: tailoring resumes effectively for each specific job application. This process is time-consuming yet crucial for navigating Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) and capturing the attention of human recruiters. The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) and associated Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools offers a potential solution, promising to streamline and enhance resume customization. However, harnessing these tools effectively requires an understanding of prompt engineering – the practice of crafting inputs that guide AI towards desired outputs. This report explores the best practices for prompt engineering in the context of resume tailoring, examines how AI tools and prompt templates can assist job seekers, and addresses the inherent challenges and limitations of this approach. The goal is to provide job seekers with a comprehensive understanding of how to leverage AI responsibly and effectively to create compelling, targeted resumes that increase their chances of securing interviews.

snippet:
What Is an Applicant Tracking System (ATS)? Here’s What You Need to Know

Are you getting rejected from jobs that you’re qualified for? The reason could be that your resume isn’t optimised for the ATS. Author: Kelsey Purcell. Optimise Your Resume.

Table of Contents
	•	What is an ATS?
	•	Who uses applicant tracking systems?
	•	What are the most commonly used ATS?
	•	How do applicant tracking systems work?
	•	What other features does an ATS have?
	•	What is the difference between an ATS and a CRM?
	•	How can you optimise your resume for the ATS?
	•	What are the most common ATS myths?
You spend hours polishing your resume, making sure every skill and accomplishment shines. But despite all that effort, the interview requests aren’t coming in—even for jobs you know you’re qualified for. So what’s going on?

It could be that your resume isn’t even making it to a hiring manager’s desk. Instead, it’s being filtered out by a recruiter using an applicant tracking system (ATS).

ATS can be used to search for resumes with specific keywords related to the job description. If your resume doesn’t include enough of these keywords, a hiring manager might never see it.

In this article, we’ll break down what an ATS is, how it works, and most importantly, how you can optimize your resume to make sure it gets into the hands of a real person.

Will your resume pass the ATS test? Scan your resume now!

name: keyword_optimisation_strategies.md.txt
snippet:
Resume Keywords and Optimization Strategies
Introduction: What are Resume Keywords?
Resume keywords are specific words and phrases that Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) and recruiters use to identify and filter candidates. These keywords are derived from job descriptions and reflect the essential skills, experience, qualifications, and attributes sought by employers.
Keywords can appear in various sections of a resume, with ATS often prioritizing:
	•	Company Values: Terms reflecting the company's mission and culture, such as "responsibility" and "sustainability."
	•	Job Titles: Specific job titles are crucial for matching candidates to roles. If your previous titles were uncommon, consider adjusting them to align with the target job description.
	•	Soft Skills: Personal attributes like "teamwork," "creativity," and "leadership qualities."
	•	Hard Skills: Practical and technical abilities, such as specific software proficiency (e.g., "Adobe Creative Suite," "Microsoft Excel," "MySQL") or use of specific equipment (e.g., "micrometer").
	•	Qualifications: Degrees, licenses, and industry-standard certifications.
	•	Software: Specific software programs relevant to the role. Ensure you use the exact and proper name of the software.
	•	Industry-Specific Skills or Language: Common terminology and jargon within a particular industry.

quantify accomplishments.md.txt
snippet:
Use Numbers and Statistics:
Instead of saying "assisted clients," state "provided direct support to 50 clients per month" or "increased client access to resources by 15%". 

Focus on Outcomes:
Instead of saying "developed a program," state "developed and implemented a program that resulted in a 20% reduction in recidivism rates". 

Highlight Specific Achievements:
Instead of saying "managed a team," state "managed a team of 10, increasing productivity by 20% within six months". 

Quantify Your Impact on Clients:
Mention the number of clients helped, the types of services provided, and the positive outcomes achieved. 

Showcase Your Skills:
Quantify your skills by mentioning the number of cases you handled, the complexity of the cases, and the types of interventions you used. 

Tailor Your Resume:
Customize your resume to match the specific requirements and keywords of each job description. 

Use Action Verbs:
Start your accomplishment statements with strong action verbs that demonstrate your accomplishments, such as "implemented," "developed," "managed," "increased," or "improved". 

Be Specific and Measurable:
Provide concrete examples of your achievements and the impact you made. 

Showcase your specialization areas:
Many social workers specialize in certain areas, such as child welfare, mental health, or substance abuse. Make sure to prominently feature your specialization(s) on your resume, as it shows your expertise and passion.
